message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Background</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Worldwide, social media traffic increased following the onset of the coronavirus disease (COVID-19) pandemic. Although the spread of COVID-19 content has been described for several social media platforms (e.g., Twitter, Facebook), little is known about how content is spread via private messaging platforms such as WhatsApp.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objective</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>In this study, we documented: (i) how WhatsApp is used to transmit COVID-19 content; (ii) the characteristics of WhatsApp users based on their usage patterns; and (iii) how usage patterns link to well-being.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>We used the experience sampling method to track day-to-day WhatsApp usage during the COVID-19 pandemic. For one week, participants reported each day the extent to which they had received, forwarded, or discussed COVID-19 content. The final dataset comprised 924 data points collected from 151 participants.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>During the week-long monitoring, most participants (143/151, 95%) reported at least one COVID-19-related use of WhatsApp. When a taxonomy was generated based on usage patterns, 1 in 10 participants (21/151, 14%) were found to have received and shared a high volume of forwarded COVID-19 content – akin to ‘super spreaders’ identified on other social media platforms. Finally, those who engaged with more COVID-19 content in their personal chats were more likely to report having COVID-19 thoughts throughout the day.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>These findings provide a rare window into discourse on private messenger platforms. In turn, this can inform risk communication strategies during the pandemic.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Tracking private WhatsApp discourse about COVID-19: A longitudinal infodemiology study in Singapore

reference.article-title: Tracking Social Media Discourse About the COVID-19 Pandemic: Development of a Public Coronavirus Twitter Data Set
